name: GATE 2027 CSE — Complete Preparation Guide
description: A complete 865-hour GATE CSE 2027 preparation plan covering all 15 subjects across 452 lectures and 79 DPPs. Enforces a strategic E-E-M-E-H-E-M-H-M-M-H-M-E-M-H difficulty rhythm. Track A (DM, LA, Calc, Prob, TOC, Compiler) at 2 hrs/lecture; Track B (DBMS, OS, C, DS, CN, COA, DL, GA) at 1.5 hrs/lecture. All DPPs and free-day PYQ sessions at 1 hr each. Target: AIR < 200, 80+ raw marks.
prerequisites: B.Tech CS/IT or equivalent undergraduate background
Ability to commit 31 hrs/week — 3 hrs/weekday + 8 hrs Saturday + 8 hrs Sunday
Access to GATE Overflow, Made Easy or ACE Academy mock test series

sections.name: All Subjects Combined (50 Qs — all Track A + B)
Week 1: DBMS + OS + C + DS + Algorithms (target ≥86%)
Week 2: CN + COA + Digital Logic + GA (tiered targets)
Week 3: Eng Math + DM + TOC + Compiler (target ≥80%)
General Aptitude — Attempt FIRST (10 Qs, 15 marks, 20 min)
Core CS — Tier 1: DBMS→OS→DSA→CN, Tier 2: Math→COA→Algo, Tier 3: Compiler→DM→TOC→DL

stages.name: Phase 3C — Final Sprint (Half-Mock, last study activity)

sections.name: Half-Mock: 33 Questions under full exam conditions

topics.name: ER model — entities (strong vs weak), attributes (simple/composite/multivalued/derived)
ER model — relationships (cardinality 1:1/1:N/M:N, participation total/partial)
Translate 2 ER diagrams to relational schema
1NF (atomic, no repeating groups); 2NF (no partial dependency on any proper subset of composite key)
3NF (no transitive dependency: non-prime depends on non-prime through non-prime)
BCNF (every determinant is a superkey — BCNF ⊂ 3NF, may lose dependency preservation)
Lossless join test: R1∩R2 → R1 or R1∩R2 → R2
DPP 1 — 10 normalization PYQs on paper
Relational algebra: σ (select rows), π (project columns), × (Cartesian product)
Relational algebra: ⋈ (natural join = ×+rename+π), ⋈θ (theta join), ÷ (division for 'for all' queries)
SQL: SELECT/FROM/WHERE, GROUP BY/HAVING, all join types (INNER/LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER/NATURAL/CROSS)
SQL: aggregate functions, correlated vs non-correlated subqueries, EXISTS/NOT EXISTS
Transactions: ACID properties; Serializability: conflict-equivalent (same conflicting pairs, same order)
Precedence graph (cycle → not conflict-serializable); View-equivalence
DPP 2 — 10 transaction + SQL PYQs on paper
2-Phase Locking: growing phase (acquire only), shrinking phase (release only)
Strict 2PL (hold all locks until commit — prevents dirty reads); Conservative 2PL (acquire all at start — no deadlock)
Indexing: B+ tree (all records in leaves, leaves linked, internal nodes have keys only)
Dense vs sparse index, multilevel indexing

Deadlock 4 necessary conditions: (1) Mutual Exclusion, (2) Hold and Wait, (3) No Preemption, (4) Circular Wait
RAG: single-instance (cycle ↔ deadlock); multi-instance (cycle necessary not sufficient)
Banker's safety check: find process with Need≤Available, run it, add Allocation to Available, repeat. Work: 3 resources (A=10,B=5,C=7), 5 processes — find safe sequence
Banker's request: (1) Request≤Need, (2) Request≤Available, (3) tentative allocate, (4) safety check → grant or rollback
Paging EAT: no TLB = 2×t_m; with TLB: h×t_m+(1−h)×(t_m+t_p). Solve 5 EAT numericals

Classical problems: producer-consumer (mutex + empty=N + full=0), readers-writers (first and second variants)

Struct padding: members aligned to their size; struct size rounds up to largest member's alignment
Union: all members share starting address, last written member valid to read; sizeof(array) vs sizeof(pointer)
Bitwise: x&(x−1) clears lowest set bit, x|(x+1) sets lowest clear bit; Macro pitfalls: SQ(2+3)=11 not 25 with #define SQ(x) x*x

AVL trees: balance factor = h(left)−h(right) ∈ {−1,0,1}; draw all 4 rotations from memory: LL (right rotate), RR (left rotate)
AVL trees: LR (left rotate left child, then right rotate), RL (right rotate right child, then left rotate); insert 5 sequences
B-trees: order m, max m−1 keys/node, min ⌈m/2⌉−1 (except root); split on overflow
Heaps: max-heap property (parent ≥ children); build-heap from array O(n) — heapify-down from n/2→1; heap sort trace
Priority queue: insert O(log n), extract-max O(log n)
Hashing: chaining (load factor α=n/m, expected successful search = 1+α/2, unsuccessful = 1+α)
Open addressing: linear probing (primary clustering), quadratic (secondary clustering), double hashing h(k,i)=(h1(k)+i×h2(k)) mod m

Ch01 L1 — Sets: types, power set, all 5 operations (draw Venn diagrams)
Ch01 L2 — Relations: Cartesian product, types (reflexive, symmetric, transitive, antisymmetric, equivalence, partial order). Check from matrix.
Ch01 L3 — Functions: injective, surjective, bijective. f∘g, inverse. Counting: n^m, P(n,m), surjections.
Ch01 L4 — Algebraic structures: semigroup, monoid, group, abelian group. Verify all 4 axioms.
DPP 1
Ch01 L5 — Subgroups, cosets, Lagrange's theorem
Ch01 L6 — Lattices: POSET, Hasse diagrams, GLB (meet ∧), LUB (join ∨)
Ch01 L7 — Boolean algebra: axioms, De Morgan, duality, canonical SOP/POS from truth table
Ch01 L8 — Proof techniques: direct, contradiction, contrapositive. Mathematical induction: base + step. Prove 3 identities.
DPP 3
Ch01 L9 — Strong induction, well-ordering, structural induction. Prove: every n>1 has a prime factor.
Ch01 L10 — Countability: N,Z,Q countable; R uncountable. Cantor's diagonalization — reproduce the proof.
Ch01 L11 — Recurrences: linear homogeneous (characteristic equation), non-homogeneous (particular solution). Solve T(n)=3T(n−1)−2T(n−2).
Ch01 L12 — Generating functions: OGF, closed forms, coin-change application
DPP 5
Ch01 L13 — EGF: sequences, permutation counting applications
Ch01 L14 — Modular arithmetic, Fermat's little theorem, Euler's theorem. CRT — solve 2 full problems.
Ch01 L15 — Normal subgroups, quotient groups, homomorphisms, isomorphism theorems (statements)
Ch01 L16 — Rings, integral domains, field hierarchy. Self-quiz: 10 questions from memory without notes.

Ch02 L2 — Paths, cycles, connectivity, strongly connected, connected components
Ch02 L3 — Trees: connected acyclic, n vertices→n−1 edges, rooted, binary, height, Cayley's formula n^(n−2)
Ch02 L4 — BFS/DFS: O(V+E), edge classification (tree/back/forward/cross), timestamps d[v] and f[v]
Ch02 DPP 1

Ch02 L6 — Bipartite: 2-colourable ↔ no odd cycles. König's theorem (min vertex cover = max matching).
Ch02 L7 — Planar graphs: Euler's formula V−E+F=2. E≤3V−6. K5 and K3,3 non-planar. Kuratowski's.
Ch02 L8 — Graph colouring: χ(G). χ(tree)=2, χ(odd cycle)=3, χ(Kn)=n. 4-colour theorem (statement).

Ch03 L2 — CNF: conjunction of clauses. DNF: disjunction of minterms. Convert any formula to CNF.

OSI 7 layers: Physical (bits, cables), Data Link (frames, MAC, error control), Network (packets, routing, IP), Transport (segments, TCP/UDP), Session, Presentation, Application (HTTP, SMTP)
TCP/IP 4-layer model
CRC by hand: divide message polynomial by generator polynomial, remainder = FCS (work 2 full examples)
Hamming distance: detect d errors → distance ≥ d+1; correct d → distance ≥ 2d+1
DPP 1 — OSI, CRC, Hamming distance problems
Flow control: stop-and-wait η=1/(1+2a); GBN (retransmit from error: window≤2^n−1 — cumulative ACK); SR (window≤2^(n-1) — receiver buffers)
Flow control: Solve 5 efficiency problems
MAC protocols: ALOHA pure+slotted (derive max throughput — Pure 18.4% at G=0.5, Slotted 36.8% at G=1)
MAC protocols: CSMA/CD — sense→transmit→collision→jam→exponential backoff. Min frame size: t_frame ≥ 2×t_prop
DPP 2 — Flow control efficiency and ALOHA throughput calculations
CIDR; Subnetting: borrow host bits, subnets=2^borrowed, hosts=2^remaining−2
VLSM: 5 problems (192.168.1.0/24 → subnets for 60/30/10/10 hosts)
DPP 3 — Subnetting and VLSM calculations on paper
Routing lectures continue at reduced pace (Wk 25 evgs only)
Routing: RIP (Bellman-Ford, distance vector, count-to-infinity, split horizon with poisoned reverse)
Routing: OSPF (link-state, Dijkstra, faster convergence); BGP (path vector, inter-AS, TCP port 179)
TCP 3-way handshake (SYN→SYN-ACK→ACK), 4-way teardown; sequence numbers: byte-based
Congestion control: slow start (cwnd doubles/RTT until ssthresh), congestion avoidance (+1 MSS/RTT), fast retransmit (3 dup-ACKs)
UDP; DNS hierarchy (root→TLD→authoritative), recursive vs iterative, record types (A/AAAA/MX/CNAME/NS); HTTP persistent vs non-persistent; SMTP (push, TCP 25)

LA L1 — Vector spaces: definition, subspaces, span, linear independence, basis, dimension
LA L2 — Row reduction: REF, RREF, rank via elementary row operations
LA L3 — Null space (Ax=0 solution set), column space, rank-nullity theorem: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n
LA L4 — Linear transformations: kernel, image, matrix representation, change of basis
LA L5 — Systems of linear equations: consistent (unique: rank=n; infinite: rank<n) vs inconsistent (rank(A)≠rank([A|b]))
LA L6 — Eigenvalues: characteristic polynomial det(A−λI)=0, compute for 2×2 and 3×3
LA L7 — Eigenvectors, eigenspaces. Algebraic multiplicity vs geometric multiplicity. AM ≥ GM always.
LA L8 — Diagonalization: A=PDP⁻¹. Conditions: n linearly independent eigenvectors. Symmetric → always diagonalizable.
LA L9 — Orthogonality: orthonormal basis, Gram-Schmidt, QR decomposition (concept)
LA L10 — Positive definite matrices: all eigenvalues > 0. Quadratic forms.
LA L11
LA L12
LA L13
LA L14 — Last Linear Algebra lecture
Free day: LA Assessment — 20 GATE PYQs timed 40 min. Target ≥75%.
Number systems: 1s complement (flip all bits), 2s complement (flip+1); overflow detection: signed overflow when carry into MSB ≠ carry out of MSB
IEEE 754 single precision: 1 sign + 8 exponent (biased 127) + 23 mantissa. Special values: 0, denormal, ±∞, NaN. Encode and decode 3 values.
Addressing modes: immediate, register, direct, indirect, register indirect, indexed, base+displacement
Performance: IC×CPI×T_clock; MIPS = f_clock/(CPI×10^6); Amdahl: Speedup = 1/((1−f)+f/k) — solve 5 problems
DPP 1 — Number systems and IEEE 754 problems on paper
DPP 2 — Performance equation and Amdahl problems
Pipelining: 5 stages IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB. Ideal CPI=1. Time=(k+n−1)×T_clock. Speedup→k for large n. Draw timing diagram for 6 instructions.
Pipeline hazards: RAW (most common — consumer reads before producer writes), WAR (only in out-of-order), WAW
Stall: insert NOP bubble; Forwarding: result from EX/MEM to EX input — eliminates most RAW stalls except load-use (1 stall still needed)
Control hazard: branch penalty = 2 stalls for 5-stage
DPP 3 — Pipeline timing diagram on paper
DPP 4 — Hazard identification and stall counting
DPP 5 — Mixed pipelining problems
Cache: direct mapping (tag|index|offset — index selects line, tag compared)
Cache: fully associative (compare all tags, LRU/FIFO replacement); k-way set-associative (tag|set index|offset). Cache capacity = sets × k × block size.
AMAT = L1 hit time + L1 miss rate × (L2 hit time + L2 miss rate × Main memory time). Solve 8 problems including write-through vs write-back cases.
DPP 6 — Cache mapping problems on paper
DPP 7 — AMAT calculations (L1+L2+MM hierarchy)
DPP 8 — Mixed cache problems
Virtual memory: VPN→PFN via page table. Single-level: 2 accesses. 2-level: VPN1+VPN2+offset, 3 accesses without TLB.
TLB EAT: h×t_m+(1−h)×(t_m+t_p). With 2-level: h×t_m+(1−h)×(2×t_p+t_m). Solve 5 EAT problems. Combined TLB+cache.
DPP 9 — Virtual memory and page table problems
DPP 10 — TLB EAT calculations on paper
I/O: programmed I/O (CPU busy-waits); interrupt-driven (CPU interrupted on completion); DMA (CPU sets up, DMA transfers directly, interrupts only on completion)

Calc L1 — Limits: ε-δ definition, L'Hôpital (0/0 and ∞/∞).
Calc L2 — Continuity: removable/jump/infinite discontinuities, IVT, differentiability → continuity (not converse).
Calc L3 — Derivatives: chain rule, product, quotient, implicit differentiation. MVT: f'(c)=(f(b)−f(a))/(b−a). Rolle's.
Calc L4 — Applications: maxima/minima (critical points), second derivative test, inflection points.
Free day 1: 10 PYQs on limits + derivatives
Calc L5 — Integration: indefinite integral, substitution, integration by parts ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du
Calc L6 — Definite integral: FTC Part 1 (d/dx∫f=f(x)), Part 2 (∫[a,b]f=F(b)−F(a)). Area between curves.
Calc L7 — Multivariable: partial derivatives, gradient ∇f=(∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y), directional derivative, critical points f(x,y).
Calc L8 — Total derivative, Jacobian matrix, chain rule for multivariable.
Free day 2: 10 PYQs on integration
Calc L9 — Double integrals: iterated, changing order, change of variables (Jacobian).
Calc L10 — Taylor series: f(x)=Σf^(n)(a)/n!×(x−a)^n. Maclaurin for e^x, sin x, cos x, ln(1+x), (1+x)^n. Radius of convergence.
Calc L11
Calc L12
Free day 3: 10 PYQs on series + multivariable
Calc L13
Calc L14
Calc L15
Calc L16 — Last Calculus lecture
Free day 4: Calc Assessment — 20 PYQs timed 40 min. Target ≥72%.
Complexity: Big-O/Theta/Omega. Growth rates: 1 < log n < √n < n < n log n < n² < n³ < 2^n < n!
Master Theorem — 3 cases (memorise the conditions cold, solve 10 recurrences in first session)
Sorting: merge sort (stable, Case 2 MT, T(n)=2T(n/2)+n); quicksort (best/avg O(n log n), worst O(n²)); heapsort (O(n log n) all cases, in-place, not stable)
Lower bound: Ω(n log n) (decision tree argument, n! leaves)
BFS O(V+E): shortest paths in unweighted. DFS O(V+E): timestamps d[v], f[v]. Edge types in directed DFS: tree, back (ancestor → cycle), forward, cross.
Topological sort: DFS, output nodes in decreasing f[v] order (DAGs only)
DPP 1 — Complexity and Master Theorem problems
DPP 2 — Sorting and graph traversal problems
DPP 3 — Mixed graph problems
SCC Kosaraju: (1) DFS on G record finish times, (2) compute G^T, (3) DFS on G^T in decreasing finish time — each DFS tree = one SCC
Dijkstra: greedy, min-priority queue, O((V+E) log V). Fails on negative edges.
Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V−1 times, O(VE). Detect negative cycles (one more round — if any relaxation possible → cycle)
Floyd-Warshall: d[i][j][k]=min(d[i][j][k−1], d[i][k][k−1]+d[k][j][k−1]), O(V³)
MST: Kruskal (sort edges, union-find, add if no cycle, O(E log E)); Prim (grow tree: add min-weight edge crossing cut, O(E log V)). Both correct by cut property.
Greedy: activity selection (sort by finish time, always pick earliest compatible); fractional knapsack (sort by value/weight); Huffman (always merge 2 lowest-frequency nodes)
DPP 4 — Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford problems
DPP 5 — Floyd-Warshall and MST problems
DPP 6 — Greedy problems on paper
DP on paper: 0/1 Knapsack (n×W table: dp[i][w]=max(dp[i−1][w], dp[i−1][w−w_i]+v_i))
DP on paper: LCS (dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1 if match); LIS (dp[i]=max dp[j]+1 for j<i, a[j]<a[i])
DP on paper: Matrix Chain Multiplication (dp[i][j]=min over k: dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]+p[i-1]×p[k]×p[j])

Prob L1 — Sample space, events, Kolmogorov axioms, classical probability
Prob L2 — Conditional probability P(A|B)=P(A∩B)/P(B), multiplication rule, independent events
Prob L3 — Bayes' theorem: P(A|B)=P(B|A)P(A)/P(B). Total probability rule. Draw probability trees — 3 full Bayes' examples.
Prob L4 — Random variables: discrete PMF, CDF F(x)=P(X≤x), E[X], Var(X)=E[X²]−(E[X])²
Free day 1: 10 Bayes' theorem PYQs
Prob L5 — Moments, MGF M_X(t)=E[e^tX]
Prob L6 — Binomial: X~B(n,p), P(X=k)=C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), mean=np, var=np(1-p)
Prob L7 — Poisson: X~Pois(λ), P(X=k)=e^(−λ)λ^k/k!, mean=var=λ. B(n,p)→Pois(np) when n large, p small.
Prob L8 — Normal: X~N(μ,σ²), Z=(X−μ)/σ, 68-95-99.7 rule
Free day 2: 10 PYQs on Binomial / Poisson / Normal
Prob L9 — Exponential: memoryless P(X>s+t|X>s)=P(X>t). Uniform X~U(a,b).
Prob L10 — Joint distributions: joint PMF/PDF, marginals, conditional, independence (joint = product of marginals)
Prob L11 — Covariance: Cov(X,Y)=E[XY]−E[X]E[Y]. Correlation ρ=Cov/√(Var(X)Var(Y)). Independent → uncorrelated (not converse).
Prob L12 — Functions of RVs: Z=X+Y (convolution), Z=max(X,Y), Z=min(X,Y)
Prob L13 — LLN (weak: X̄→μ in probability), CLT: √n(X̄−μ)/σ → N(0,1)
Prob L14 — Markov chains: state space, transition matrix P, n-step P^n, classification
Prob L15 — Steady-state distribution π: πP=π, Σπ_i=1. Ergodic chains have unique steady state.
Prob L16
Free day 3: 10 PYQs on Markov chains
Prob L17
Prob L18
Prob L19

DPP 20 — Final GA DPP on paper
CL1 — Phases: 6 phases (lexical, syntax, semantic, IR gen, optimization, code gen). Symbol table, error recovery.
CL2 — Lexical analysis: tokens, lexeme, patterns. RE for tokens. LEX tool structure.
CL3 — FIRST sets: FIRST(terminal)={terminal}. FIRST(ε-production) includes ε. FIRST(A)=FIRST of all RHS. Compute for 2 grammars fully.
CL4 — FOLLOW sets: FOLLOW(S)={$}. For A→αBβ: FOLLOW(B)⊇FIRST(β)−{ε}. If ε∈FIRST(β): FOLLOW(B)⊇FOLLOW(A). Compute for same 2 grammars.
Free day 1: 10 FIRST+FOLLOW PYQs — timed
CL5 — LL(1) conditions: no left recursion, no common prefixes, FIRST(α)∩FIRST(β)=∅, if ε∈FIRST(α): FIRST(α)∩FOLLOW(A)=∅
CL6 — LL(1) parse table: add A→α to table[A][a] for each a∈FIRST(α). If ε∈FIRST(α), also add for b∈FOLLOW(A). Parse 1 string fully.
CL7 — LR(0) items: dotted productions. Closure (add items for dot-before-nonterminal). Goto. Build LR(0) automaton.
CL8 — SLR table: states from LR(0) automaton. Shift on goto. Reduce A→α● in state: add to all a∈FOLLOW(A).
DPP 1 — LL(1) parse table + SLR automaton on paper
CL9 — LALR(1): merge CLR(1) states with identical cores, combine lookaheads. More powerful than SLR, less than CLR(1).
CL10 — CLR(1): full canonical LR. Items are (production, lookahead) pairs. Closure with lookahead propagation. Work 1 full CLR example.
CL11 — Conflicts: shift-reduce (ambiguous grammar), reduce-reduce (same lookahead). Operator precedence resolves dangling else.
CL12 — Semantic analysis: attribute grammars. Synthesized (children→parent: bottom-up) vs inherited (parent→children: top-down).
DPP 2 — LALR vs CLR conflicts identification on paper
CL13 — S-attributed: only synthesized. Evaluated during LR parsing (single bottom-up pass).
CL14 — L-attributed: inherited attributes flow left-to-right. Every S-attributed is L-attributed.
CL15 — SDT: syntax-directed translation schemes, embedding actions in grammar productions
CL16
Free day 2: SLR + LALR practice on 2 new grammars (timed)
CL17 — Intermediate code: 3-address code x=y op z. TAC. Quadruples vs Triples.
CL18 — Basic blocks (maximal straight-line code). Control flow graph: nodes=basic blocks, edges=control flow.
CL19 — Local optimization: constant folding (3+4→7), dead code elimination, CSE.
CL20
Free day 3: Compiler DPP 3
DPP 3 — TAC tracing and optimization on paper
CL21
CL22
CL23 — ⚠️ DO NOT SKIP: Data flow analysis. LIVE VARIABLE ANALYSIS: variable v is live at point p if there exists a path from p to a use of v without redefinition. Used in register allocation.
CL24 — Loop optimization: code motion (loop-invariant computation moved outside), strength reduction (x*2→x+x), induction variable elimination
CL25
CL26–CL28 — Code generation, register allocation concepts (complete)
Free day 4: Compiler timed drill — FIRST/FOLLOW for 2 new grammars. SLR table for 1 grammar. Target: FIRST/FOLLOW < 8 min, SLR zero errors.
TOC L1 — DFA: formal 5-tuple (Q,Σ,δ,q0,F), δ*, language of a DFA
TOC L2 — DFA construction: even 0s, strings ending in 01, divisibility by 3 — draw complete state diagram for each
TOC L3 — DFA minimization: table-filling algorithm (Myhill-Nerode). Work 1 full example with 6+ states.
TOC L4 — NFA: formal def, ε-transitions, ε-closure, language equivalence with DFA
DPP 1 — DFA/NFA construction and minimization on paper
TOC L5 — NFA→DFA: subset construction. Work through 1 complete example — draw all reachable states.
TOC L6 — Regular Expressions: Kleene star, concatenation, union. RE→DFA (Thompson's concept). DFA→RE (state elimination).
TOC L7 — Kleene's theorem: RE = NFA = DFA. RL closure: union, concatenation, star, complement, intersection.
TOC L8 — Pumping Lemma for RL. Work through 3 full non-regularity proofs.
TOC L9 — CFG: 4-tuple (V,Σ,R,S), derivations (leftmost, rightmost), parse trees, yield
TOC L10 — Ambiguity: ≥2 parse trees for same string. Inherent ambiguity.
TOC L11 — CFG simplification: remove ε-productions, unit productions, useless symbols. CNF: A→BC or A→a.
TOC L12 — GNF: A→aα. CYK algorithm for parsing O(n³) — concept.
TOC L13 — PDA: 6-tuple, stack operations, configurations. Acceptance by final state vs empty stack.
TOC L14 — Deterministic PDA: deterministic ↔ DCFL. CFL ⊃ DCFL. L={ww^R} is CFL but not DCFL.
TOC L15 — CFL Pumping Lemma: uvxyz, |vxy|≤p, |vy|≥1. Prove {a^n b^n c^n} not CFL.
Free day 1: 25 GATE DFA/NFA/CFG PYQs timed 45 min
TOC L16 — CFL closure: closed under union, concatenation, star. NOT closed under intersection, complement.
TOC L17 — TM: 7-tuple, tape alphabet Γ⊃Σ, transition δ:Q×Γ→Q×Γ×{L,R}, accept/reject/loop
TOC L18 — TM examples: binary increment, unary addition, palindrome recognition
TOC L19 — TM variants: multi-tape (same power), NDTM, 2-stack PDA. All equivalent to standard TM.
DPP 2 — CFG derivation, PDA states, and TM problems on paper
TOC L20 — Church-Turing thesis. R (recursive) languages: TM always halts. RE languages: TM may loop on non-members.
TOC L21 — Halting problem: undecidable. Proof by diagonalization — reproduce the proof from scratch.
TOC L22 — Rice's theorem: any non-trivial semantic property of TM languages is undecidable. Apply: 'does TM accept ε?'
TOC L23 — Post Correspondence Problem: undecidable. Used as basis for other undecidability proofs.
DPP 3 — Decidability and reduction mapping problems on paper

TOC L24–L26 — P and NP: P (decided in poly time), NP (verified in poly time), NP-hard, NP-complete. Cook's theorem (SAT is NP-complete). Reduction chain: SAT → 3-SAT → Vertex Cover → Independent Set → Clique → Hamiltonian Path → TSP. Know this chain cold.
TOC L27
TOC L28
TOC L29 — Last TOC lecture
